evidence_quotes.quote: I can not play this game ever again because they shut down the servers.
But after the server shutdown in 2016, even solo, it can not be played.
Secondly, payment processors such as Visa and Mastercard have an undue level of power.
They recently put pressure on Steam and Itch.IO to remove certain kinds of adult games from their platforms.
what happens when such payment processors gain too much power and can control what kind of media the citizens of the EU can and can not consume?
it is not a democratic thing to spy on your citizens, forcing them to use their digital or photo IDs to prove who they are just to view certain content online.
The fact that this is a step governments in the EU are taking is appalling and undemocratic.

paragraphs.text: Good day, I would like to write about three particular issues that I think this law can help with in many ways if they're added in. First, the issue of video games being destroyed. Yes, there is a citizens' initiative. But I view that this law absolutely fits with the Stop Killing video games' mission statement. Just as a personal example. The game Darkspore. I bought it in 2011 with my own money. I still own the disc. I can not play this game ever again because they shut down the servers. It could be played online with friends or solo. But after the server shutdown in 2016, even solo, it can not be played. Please look towards Stop Killing Video Games and its mission because I should be able to buy a product and use it as I see fit. Now, not all currently shut-down games are expected to suddenly get support, but at the very least, they'd like to make sure that events like Darkspore never happen again. Secondly, payment processors such as Visa and Mastercard have an undue level of power. They recently put pressure on Steam and Itch.IO to remove certain kinds of adult games from their platforms. Some of those games were legitimate to remove, I'd say. However, what happens when such payment processors gain too much power and can control what kind of media the citizens of the EU can and can not consume? Thirdly and lastly, I'd like to make a note that it is not a democratic thing to spy on your citizens, forcing them to use their digital or photo IDs to prove who they are just to view certain content online. This is in response to an Irish law that came into effect recently. The Irish Online Safety Code. It is currently affecting other EU citizens, not just Irish ones, who use Twitter/X due to its wording. While for me, as a Swedish citizen, it only affects me on certain kinds of content on Twitter/X. The fact that this is a step governments in the EU are taking is appalling and undemocratic. I understand the need to protect children; however, it should not come at the expense of adults who are free to do as they please. Thank you for your time reading this, and I hope some of this helps get added to the law or at least taken into consideration to help make the internet a fairer place.
